content:
# Real-world benchmark: combined LUT GPU grading pipeline (post-PR #33)

**Date:** 2026-04-03
**Pipeline version:** after issue #28 / PR #33 — combined 97³ CUDA 3D texture LUT replacing 3-kernel pipeline

## Test clip

- File: `DJI_20251101111428_0055_D.MP4`
- Resolution: 3840×2160 (4K), HEVC 10-bit
- Frame rate: 119.88 fps
- Duration: 13.95 s → 1,671 frames

## Results

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total wall time | **2 min 6.859 s** |
| User time | 5 min 31.739 s |
| Sys time | 0 min 56.995 s |
| Output size | 202 MB |
| Output path | `/tmp/DJI_20251101111428_0055_D_graded.mp4` |

## Approximate time breakdown

| Stage | Time |
|-------|------|
| Auto-calibration (20 keyframes × RAUNE-Net + Depth Anything V2) | ~35 s |
| Per-frame depth inference (keyframes) + temporal interpolation + GPU grading | ~1 min 49 s |
| GPU grading kernel only (~3 ms/frame × 1,671 frames) | < 5 s |

## Key finding

**GPU grading is no longer the pipeline bottleneck.** The combined LUT kernel runs at ~3 ms/frame (vs. ~332 ms with the old 3-kernel pipeline — ~100× speedup). The bottleneck is now depth inference subprocess invocations per keyframe.

## Next optimization target

Depth inference (Depth Anything V2 subprocess, called per keyframe) dominates wall time. Batching keyframes into a single subprocess call or moving to an in-process inference model would be the highest-leverage improvement.
